ITEM 1.  BUSINESS
In this report, the terms "The Coca-Cola Company," "Company," "we," "us" and "our" mean The Coca-Cola Company and all entities included in our consolidated financial statements.
General
The Coca-Cola Company is a total beverage company, and beverage products bearing our trademarks, sold in the United States since 1886, are now sold in more than 200 countries and territories. We own or license and market numerous beverage brands, which we group into the following categories: Trademark Coca-Cola; sparkling flavors; water, sports, coffee and tea; juice, value-added dairy and plant-based beverages; and emerging beverages. We own and market several of the world's largest nonalcoholic sparkling soft drink brands, including Coca-Cola, Sprite, Coca-Cola Zero Sugar, Fanta and Diet Coke/Coca-Cola Light.
We make our branded beverage products available to consumers throughout the world through our network of independent bottling partners, distributors, wholesalers and retailers as well as our consolidated bottling and distribution operations. Beverages bearing trademarks owned by or licensed to the Company account for 2.2 billion of the estimated 65 billion servings of all beverages consumed worldwide every
…[truncated 20785 chars]…
any has also entered into manufacturing agreements that authorize certain EPBs that have executed EPB CBAs to manufacture certain beverage products for their own account and for supply to other bottlers.
In addition, certain U.S. bottlers that were not granted additional exclusive territory rights, which we refer to as "participating bottlers," converted their legacy bottler's agreements to CBAs, to which we refer as "participating bottler CBAs," each of which has a term of 10 years, is renewable by the bottler indefinitely for successive additional terms of 10 years each, and is substantially similar in most material respects to the EPB CBAs, including with respect to requirements for a binding national governance model and mandatory incidence pricing, but includes core performance requirements that vary in certain respects from those in the EPB CBAs.
Those bottlers that have not signed CBAs continue to operate under legacy bottler's agreements that include pricing formulas that generally provide for a baseline price for Trademark Coca-Cola Beverages and other cola-flavored Company Trademark Beverages. This baseline price may be adjusted periodically by the Company, up to a maximum indexed ceiling price, and is adjusted quarterly based upon changes in certain sugar or sweetener prices, as applicable. The U.S. unit case volume prepared, packaged, sold and distributed under these legacy bottler's agreements is not material.
Under the terms of the bottler's agreements, bottlers in the United States generally are not authorized to manufacture fountain syrups. Rather, the Company manufactures and sells fountain syrups to authorized fountain wholesalers (including certain authorized bottlers) and some fountain retailers. These wholesalers in turn sell the syrups, or deliver them on our behalf, to restaurants and other retailers.
6
Promotional and Marketing Programs
In addition to conducting our own independent advertising and marketing activities, we may provide promotional and marketing support and/or funds to our bottlers. In most cases, we do this on a discretionary basis under the terms of commitment letters or agreements, even though we are not obligated to do so under the terms of the bottler's agreements between our Company and the bottlers. Also, on a discretionary basis in most cases, our Company may develop and introduce new products, packages and equipment to assist the bottlers. Likewise, in many instances, we provide promotional and marketing support and/or funds and/or dispensing equipment and repair services to fountain and bottle/can retailers, typically pursuant to marketing agreements.
Investments in Bottling Operations
Most of our branded beverage products are prepared, packaged, distributed and sold by independent bottling partners. However, from time to time we acquire or take control of a bottling operation, often in underperforming markets where we believe we can use our resources and expertise to improve performance. Owning a bottling operation enables us to compensate for limited local resources; help focus the bottler's sales and marketing programs; assist in the development of the bottler's business and information systems; and establish an appropriate capital structure for the bottler. In line with our long-term bottling strategy, we may periodically consider options for divesting or reducing our ownership interest in a consolidated bottling operation, typically by selling all or a portion of our interest in the bottling operation to an independent bottler to improve Coca-Cola system efficiency. When we sell a consolidated bottling operation to an independent bottling partner in which we have an equity method investment, our Company continues to participate in the bottler's results of operations through our share of the equity method investee's earnings or losses.
In addition, from time to time we make equity investments representing noncontrolling interests in certain bottling operations with the intention of maximizing the strength and efficiency of the Coca-Cola system's production, marketing, sales and distribution capabilities around the world by providing expertise and resources to strengthen those businesses. These investments are intended to result in increases in unit case volume, net operating revenues and profits at the bottler level, which in turn generate increased sales for our Company's concentrate operations. When our equity investment provides us with the ability to exercise significant influence over the investee bottler's operating and financial policies, we account for the investment under the equity method.
Seasonality
Sales of our ready-to-drink beverages are somewhat seasonal, with the second and third calendar quarters historically accounting for the highest sales volumes. The volume of sales in the beverage business may be affected by weather conditions.
Competition
The commercial beverage industry is highly competitive and consists of numerous companies, ranging from small or emerging to very large and well established. These include companies that, like our Company, compete globally in multiple geographic areas, as well as businesses that are primarily regional or local in operation. Competitive products include numerous nonalcoholic sparkling soft drinks; water products, including flavored and enhanced waters; juices, juice drinks and nectars; dilutables (including syrups and powders); coffees; teas; energy drinks; sports drinks; milk and other dairy-based drinks; plant-based beverages; functional beverages, including vitamin-based products and relaxation beverages; and various other nonalcoholic beverages. These competitive products are sold to consumers in both ready-to-drink and non-ready-to-drink form. The Company has directly entered the alcohol beverage category in numerous markets outside the United States. In the United States, the Company has established a wholly owned, indirect, firewalled subsidiary, which uses third-party manufacturers and distributors to produce and sell alcohol products in certain regions of the United States and also authorizes alcohol-licensed third parties to use certain of our trademarks and related intellectual property on alcohol beverages that contain Company beverage bases. Competitive products include all alcohol ready-to-drink beverages containing various alcohol bases. In many of the countries in which we do business, PepsiCo, Inc. is a primary competitor. Other significant competitors include, but are not limited to, Nestlé S.A., Keurig Dr Pepper Inc., Danone S.A., Suntory Beverage & Food Limited, Anheuser-Busch InBev, Kirin Holdings, Heineken N.V., Diageo plc and Red Bull GmbH. We also compete against numerous regional and local companies and, increasingly, against smaller companies that are developing microbrands and selling them directly to consumers through  e-commerce retailers and other e-commerce platforms. In addition, in some markets, we compete against retailers that have developed their own store or private-label beverage brands.
Competitive factors impacting our business include, but are not limited to, pricing, advertising, sales promotion programs,     in-store displays and point-of-sale marketing, digital marketing, product and ingredient innovation, availability, increased efficiency in production techniques, the introduction of new packaging as well as new vending and dispensing equipment, contracting with marketing assets (theaters, sports arenas, universities, etc.), and brand and trademark development and protection.

## KO — Quantitative Report (Decision Date 2025-10-01)

---

### 1. Revenue, Earnings, Margin, Cash-Flow & Balance-Sheet Trajectory

**Revenue** is essentially flat on a YoY basis: Q2 2025 revenue of $12.54B is only +1.4% above Q2 2024's $12.36B. TTM revenue stands at $47.06B. The QoQ jump of +12.6% (from Q1's $11.13B) reflects normal seasonal pattern rather than acceleration.

**Earnings** have expanded dramatically despite flat revenue. Q2 2025 EPS of $0.88 is +57.1% YoY versus Q2 2024's $0.56. TTM net income is $12.20B. The EPS trajectory over eight quarters: 0.71 → 0.46 → 0.74 → 0.56 → 0.66 → 0.51 → 0.77 → 0.88, showing a clear upward step-change in the two most recent quarters.

**Margins** have expanded sharply. Q2 2025 operating income of $4.28B implies an operating margin of 34.1%, versus only 21.3% in Q2 2024 ($2.63B/$12.36B). Net margin in Q2 2025 is 30.3% ($3.80B/$12.54B) versus 19.4% a year earlier. TTM operating margin is 28.0% ($13.16B/$47.06B) and TTM net margin is 25.9%. Gross margin is stable in a 60–63% band across all eight quarters.

**Cash flow** data is entirely unavailable across all supplied periods. Free cash flow is marked n/a in every instance, including the TTM panel.

**Balance sheet**: Total equity has grown from $27.48B (2023-12-31) to $30.18B (2025-06-27), a +9.8% increase. Cash stands at $12.25B, down from $17.40B in Q2 2024. Total liabilities are $74.15B against $104.33B in assets, yielding an equity ratio of 28.9%. Historical total debt (last supplied 2024-03-29) was $36.50B. Note: several intermediate quarters show clearly erroneous negative liability figures (e.g., 2025-03-28: −$27.75B), which are data-quality artifacts and excluded from analysis.

---

### 2. Earnings Quality & Balance-Sheet Resilience

**Earnings quality — mixed signals.** The +57.1% YoY EPS growth on only +1.4% revenue growth is driven entirely by operating margin expansion (from ~21% to ~34%). This is a very large swing for a mature consumer-staples company. Gross margin was essentially stable (62.4% vs 61.1%), so the improvement originates below the gross-profit line — potentially from lower SG&A, restructuring benefits, or one-time items. Without cash-flow or segment data, it is impossible to confirm whether this margin expansion is structural or transient. The Q1 2024 quarter showed a similar anomaly: net margin of 28.2% on an operating margin of only 18.9%, suggesting non-operating gains inflated that quarter's bottom line.

**Balance-sheet resilience** is adequate but not exceptional. The equity ratio of 28.9% and cash of $12.25B provide a buffer, though cash has declined by $5.15B from the Q2 2024 peak of $17.40B. With no cash-flow data, the source of this cash drain (debt repayment, dividends, buybacks, capex) cannot be determined. Total liabilities-to-equity of 2.46x ($74.15B/$30.18B) is elevated but typical for a company of KO's credit profile.

---

### 3. Valuation Versus Supplied History

| Metric | Latest (2025-09-30) | 12-Month Range | Oct 2024 Level | Implied Change |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| P/E diluted | 23.5x | 23.2x – 30.0x | 29.1x | −19.3% compression |
| P/B | 9.5x | 9.3x – 12.1x | 11.3x | −16.1% compression |
| P/S | 6.1x | n/a | n/a | — |
| Earnings yield | 4.3% | n/a | n/a | — |
| ROE | 40.4% | 0.4x–0.4x | 38.9% | +150bp expansion |
| Net margin | 25.9% | 0.20–0.30 | 22.9% | +300bp expansion |

The P/E of 23.5x sits at the **floor** of its supplied 12-month range (23.2x–30.0x). P/B of 9.5x is also near its trough (9.3x–12.1x). This compression has occurred while ROE has improved from 38.9% to 40.4% and net margin from 22.9% to 25.9%. On a pure multiple-versus-fundamentals basis, the stock is cheaper relative to its own recent history on every supplied ratio, and the improvement in returns on equity has not been rewarded.

---

### 4. Price Level & Return Context Versus Fundamentals

| Period | Price | Return |
|---|---|---|
| Latest (2025-09-30) | $66.32 | — |
| 52-week high | $74.38 | −10.8% from high |
| 52-week low | $60.62 | +9.4% from low |
| Quarterly (from 2025-07-01) | $71.67 | **−7.5%** |
| Annual (from 2024-09-26) | $71.40 | **−7.1%** |

The stock has declined −7.1% over the past year and −7.5% over the most recent quarter, while TTM EPS has expanded substantially. The quarterly path shows high volatility: +14.5% in Q4 2024, then −12.4%, +0.1%, and −7.5% in subsequent quarters. The price is currently closer to the 52-week low than the high, and the P/E compression from ~29x to ~23.5x means the market has de-rated the stock even as reported earnings improved. This is a clear divergence between price trajectory and fundamental trajectory.

---

### 5. Strongest Quantitative Upside & Downside

**Upside case (quantitative):**
- P/E reversion to the 12-month median (~27x) on current TTM EPS of ~$3.02 (=$12.20B/4.04B diluted shares implied by Q2 EPS math) would imply a price near $81.50, representing approximately **+23%** from $66.32.
- The margin expansion from ~21% operating margin to ~34% operating margin, if sustained, represents a structural step-change in profitability that the current P/E at the range floor does not reflect.
- ROE of 40.4% is at the top of its supplied range, supporting a premium multiple.

**Downside case (quantitative):**
- The 52-week low of $60.62 is only **−8.6%** below the current price; a retest is plausible if the margin expansion proves non-recurring.
- If operating margins revert to the ~21% average of the four quarters ending Q4 2024, TTM operating income would fall from $13.16B to roughly $10B, compressing net income and EPS significantly — potentially back toward the $2.20–$2.40 annual EPS range, which at 23.5x would imply a price near $52–$57.
- Cash declined $5.15B over four quarters with no visibility into free cash flow; if the company is burning cash or over-distributing, balance-sheet flexibility erodes.
- The P/E floor of 23.2x offers limited technical support; a break below would require re-rating to levels not seen in the supplied data.

---

### 6. Provisional Quantitative Risk/Reward Conclusion

KO presents a **divergence between improving fundamentals and a declining price/multiple**. The P/E of 23.5x is at the bottom of its 12-month range (23.2x–30.0x) while TTM net margin has expanded to 25.9% and ROE to 40.4%. The stock has returned −7.1% annually despite a +57.1% YoY EPS increase in the latest quarter.

The central uncertainty is **earnings durability**: the jump from ~21% to ~34% quarterly operating margin is unusually large for a staples company, and without cash-flow data, the quality of this improvement cannot be verified. If the margin expansion is structural, the current valuation embeds meaningful upside to the stock's own history. If it is transient, the downside to the 52-week low of $60.62 (−8.6%) is modest relative to the potential reversion in earnings.

**Quantitative risk/reward is asymmetric to the upside** on the supplied evidence, contingent on the margin expansion proving durable. The missing cash-flow data is the single largest evidentiary gap and prevents a high-conviction conclusion.
KO.qualitative_report:
# Qualitative Assessment: Coca-Cola Company (KO)
**Decision Date: 2025-10-01**

---

## 1. Business/Franchise Description

Per the 2025-02-20 filing (10-K, period ended 2024-12-31), Coca-Cola is a total beverage company selling branded products in 200+ countries and territories, accounting for 2.2 billion of ~65 billion daily global beverage servings. The portfolio spans sparkling soft drinks (69% of worldwide unit case volume in 2024), water, sports, coffee, tea, juice, dairy/plant-based beverages, and emerging beverages. Trademark Coca-Cola alone represents 47% of worldwide volume.

The company operates primarily as a **concentrate/franchise model**, selling syrups and concentrates to independent bottling partners (the five largest represented 44% of 2024 volume), supplemented by consolidated bottling operations (Bottling Investments segment). Geographic segments cover Europe/Middle East/Africa, Latin America, North America, Asia Pacific, plus Global Ventures and Bottling Investments. The system sold 33.7 billion unit cases in 2024 (up from 33.3 billion in 2023).

Competitive advantages include globally recognized brands, a vast bottler/distributor network, sophisticated marketing, and pricing power. Primary competitors noted include PepsiCo, Nestlé, Keurig Dr Pepper, Danone, Suntory, and an increasing number of smaller/microbrand companies selling direct-to-consumer via e-commerce.

A structural change was announced in November 2024: the Global Ventures segment is being sunset effective January 1, 2025, with Costa, innocent, and doğadan folding into the EMEA segment, and Costa RTD and Monster fees moving to respective geographic segments.

---

## 2. Recent Developments (Dated)

**Pricing Power & Financial Dynamics:**
- **2025-Q2 (evidence dated 2025-08-18 and 2025-09-05):** A 6% price/mix increase in Q2 2025 highlighted pricing power, though this accompanied volume declines. Free cash flow was noted as having "declined significantly in 2024" due to working capital and capital expenditure pressures (per Monexa analysis, 2025-08-18). Organic revenue growth of 5% YoY with margin expansion was reported for Q2 2025 (TradingView, 2025-09-05).

**Technical/Market Sentiment:**
- **2025-08-13:** KO moved above its 50-day SMA, interpreted as strong technical momentum reflecting sustained investor confidence (Yahoo Finance).

**Dividend & Valuation Narrative:**
- Multiple sources (2025-09-15 through 2025-09-29) consistently characterize KO as a "Dividend King" with a 63-year streak of dividend increases and a yield around 3%. However, one contrarian article (2025-09-28) argues limited growth potential, a lower yield vs. competitors, and S&P 500 underperformance make it unattractive despite the streak.

**Competitive/Industry Pressures:**
- **2025-09-12:** PepsiCo faces sector pressures, potential GLP-1 drug impact, and margin concerns, with activist investor Elliott pushing for changes — contextually relevant to KO's peer set (though not a direct KO development).
- **2025-09-29 (cbonds.com):** Article flagged rising beverage competition as a sustainability question for Coca-Cola's growth.

**Legal & Regulatory Risks (confirmed from text):**
- **2025-09-23:** A class-action lawsuit alleges deceptive marketing regarding "100% Natural Flavors" claims (MMR Strategy).
- **2025-09-01:** Multiple lawsuits in 2025 covering false advertising and related issues (Lawgud).
- **2025-09-24:** An $18 billion tax case — a Bloomberg Tax article questioned whether investors understand the stakes, referencing disputes over intangible valuation methodology under Section 482 regulations.
- **2025-09-22:** Scandals sparking calls to revoke Coca-Cola's license in central Colombia (Colombia Reports).

**Structural/Strategic:**
- The January 2025 segment sunset (Global Ventures) represents a streamlining effort, consistent with management's focus on simplification and scaling.

---

## 3. Material Qualitative Risks and Offsets

| Risk | Evidence | Offset |
|------|----------|--------|
| **Limited organic volume growth** | Q2 2025 price/mix up 6% but volume declined (2025-08-18); 2024 system volume only modestly higher than 2023 (10-K) | Pricing power demonstrated; organic revenue still grew 5% YoY (2025-09-05) |
| **GLP-1 / health-conscious consumer trends** | Peer PepsiCo flagged for GLP-1 risk (2025-09-12); 10-K acknowledges health trends | Diversification beyond sugared sparkling (water, tea, dairy, zero-sugar); brand adaptability cited (2025-09-27) |
| **Elevated litigation exposure** | $18B tax case (2025-09-24); "100% Natural Flavors" class action (2025-09-23); multiple 2025 lawsuits (2025-09-01); Colombia license risk (2025-09-22) | Scale and legal resources; none of these are confirmed as resolved or quantified as material to financials in supplied text |
| **Free cash flow pressure** | FCF declined significantly in 2024 (2025-08-18) | Concentrate model is capital-light by nature; pricing and margin expansion partially offset (2025-09-05) |
| **Intense and broadening competition** | 10-K names PepsiCo, Nestlé, KDP, plus microbrands and private label; e-commerce disintermediation | Unmatched global distribution network; brand recognition; 2.2B daily servings scale |
| **S&P 500 underperformance** | Cited as a reason not to buy (2025-09-28); no longer among top-3 market cap names (2025-09-12) | Frames KO more as income/defensive holding than growth compounder |

---

## 4. Qualitative Assessment

**Neutral, with a defensive/income tilt.**

The franchise remains exceptional — unmatched global brand portfolio, pricing power in concentrate operations, and a long runway of dividend reliability. The Q2 2025 results confirm the ability to grow revenue through price/mix even amid soft volumes. However, the evidence reveals no clear near-term catalyst for fundamental reacceleration: volumes are essentially flat, FCF has weakened, litigation risk is elevated (including a potentially material $18B tax dispute), and the stock has underperformed broader indices. The narrative is dominated by income/dividend commentary rather than growth, consistent with a mature, defensive holding. Competitive pressures from health trends, GLP-1 drugs, and microbrands represent structural headwinds that the diversification strategy is designed to address, but the pace of adaptation remains an open question.

**Key qualitative stance:** Franchise quality is high; business momentum is modest; risk skew from litigation and volume trends creates a balanced outlook.

The stock's −7.1% annual return despite +57% YoY EPS growth creates a mild asymmetric setup, but insufficient evidence quality caps conviction.

Item 1.    Business
General
Verizon Communications Inc. (the Company) is a holding company that, acting through its subsidiaries (together with the Company, collectively, Verizon), is one of the world's leading providers of communications, technology, information and streaming products and services to consumers, businesses and gov
…[truncated 21685 chars]…
es, we have entered into intra-market spectrum swaps designed to increase the amount of contiguous spectrum within frequency bands in a specific market. Contiguous spectrum improves network performance and efficiency. These swaps, as well as any spectrum purchases, require us to obtain governmental approvals.
Information regarding spectrum license transactions, including our pending acquisition of select spectrum licenses of United States Cellular Corporation and certain of its subsidiaries, is presented in Note 3 to the consolidated financial statements included in this Annual Report.
Competition and Related Trends
The telecommunications industry is highly competitive. The rapid development of new technologies, services and products has eliminated many of the distinctions among wireless, cable, internet and traditional telephone services and brought new competitors to our markets. We expect competition to remain intense as traditional and non-traditional participants seek increased market share.
Competitive factors within the telecommunications industry include, among others, network reliability, speed, capacity and coverage; pricing and promotional strategies; the quality of customer service; marketing, sales and distribution capabilities; development of new and enhanced products and services; the ability to anticipate and respond to various factors and trends affecting our industry; the availability of capital resources; and regulatory changes.
Competition remains intense as a result of various factors, including aggressive pricing, increased levels of promotions and service plan discounts, and offerings that include additional bundled premium content or other perks, in some cases specifically targeting Verizon customers. Competition may increase as smaller, stand-alone wireless service providers merge or transfer licenses to larger, better capitalized wireless service providers and as MVNOs resell wireless communication services. In addition, aggressive network deployment as well as increasing government incentives related to it may enhance the ability of certain of our competitors to compete with us.
With respect to our wireless connectivity products and services, we compete against other national wireless service providers, including AT&T Inc. and T-Mobile US, Inc., as well as various regional wireless service providers. We also compete for retail activations with resellers that buy bulk wholesale service from wireless service providers, including Verizon, and resell it to their customers. Resellers include cable companies, such as Comcast Corporation and Charter Communications, Inc., and others. Several major cable operators also offer bundles with wireless services through strategic relationships.
We also face competition from other communications and technology companies seeking to increase their brand recognition and capture market share with respect to the provision of wireless products and services, in addition to non-traditional offerings in mobile data. For example, Microsoft Corporation, Alphabet Inc., Apple Inc., Meta Platforms, Inc. and others are offering alternative means for messaging and making wireless voice calls that, in certain cases, can be used in lieu of the wireless providers' voice service, as well as alternative means of accessing video content. In addition, we are seeing increasing competition in the provisioning of internet access by low Earth orbit satellite companies as well in the area of fixed wireless offerings that provide an alternative to traditional landline internet service providers.
With respect to Fios and our other broadband services, we compete against cable companies, wireless service providers, domestic and foreign telecommunications providers, satellite television companies, low Earth orbit satellite companies, internet service providers, OTT providers and other companies that offer network services and managed enterprise solutions. We also

face increasing competition from other internet portal providers. We expect the market will continue to shift from traditional linear video to OTT offerings. We also expect customer migration from traditional voice services to wireless services to continue.
Companies with a global presence are increasingly competing with us in our Business segment. A relatively small number of telecommunications and integrated service providers with global operations serve customers in the global enterprise market and, to a lesser extent, the global wholesale market. We compete with these providers for large contracts to provide integrated solutions to global enterprises and government customers. Many of these companies have strong market presence, brand recognition and existing customer relationships, all of which contribute to intense competition.
In the Enterprise and Public Sector markets, competition levels remain high, primarily as a result of increased industry focus on technology convergence. We compete in this area with system integrators, carriers, and hardware and software providers. In addition, some of the largest information technology services companies are making strategic acquisitions, divesting non-strategic assets and forging new alliances to improve their cost structure. Many new alliances and acquisitions have focused on developing fields, such as cloud computing, software defined networking, communication applications and other computing tasks via networks, rather than by the use of in-house machines.
In the Business Markets and Other category, customer purchasing behaviors and preferences continue to evolve. Solution speed and simplicity are becoming key differentiators for customers who are seeking full life-cycle offers that simplify the process of starting, running and growing their businesses. Mobile network operators and other carriers are aggressively targeting this customer group with a variety of bundles and discounts as it is among the largest and fastest growing categories in the Business segment.
Our Wholesale business competes with traditional carriers for metro/mid/long-haul fiber, voice and IP services. In addition, mobile video and data needs are driving a greater need for wireless backhaul. Network providers, cable companies and specialty firms are competitors for this business opportunity.
Regulatory Trends
Regulatory Landscape
Verizon operates in a regulated and highly competitive market, as described above. Some of our competitors are subject to fewer regulatory constraints than Verizon. For many services offered by Verizon, the FCC is our primary regulator. The FCC has jurisdiction over interstate telecommunications services and other matters under the Communications Act of 1934, as amended (Communications Act). Other Verizon services are subject to various state and local regulations.
Federal, State and Local Regulation
Wireless Services
The FCC regulates several aspects of our wireless operations. Generally, the FCC has jurisdiction over the construction, operation, acquisition and transfer of wireless communications systems. All wireless services require use of radio frequency spectrum, the assignment and distribution of which is subject to FCC oversight. If demand continues to increase or if new spectrum is required for a future generation of technology, we can meet our needs for licensed spectrum by purchasing licenses or leasing spectrum from others, or by participating in a competitive bidding process to acquire new spectrum from the FCC. Those processes are subject to certain reviews, approvals and potential conditions.
Today, Verizon holds FCC spectrum licenses that allow it to provide a wide range of mobile and fixed communications services, including both voice and data services. FCC spectrum licenses typically have a term of 10 years, at which time they are subject to renewal. While the FCC has routinely renewed all of Verizon's wireless licenses, challenges co

VZ.quantitative_report:
**VZ Quantitative Assessment (Decision Date: 2025-10-01)**

**1. Revenue, Earnings, Margin, Cash-Flow, and Balance-Sheet Trajectory**
- **Revenue:** Showing clear growth. Q2 2025 revenue was 34.50B, up +3.0% QoQ from 33.48B and +5.2% YoY from 32.80B. TTM revenue stands at 134.12B.
- **Earnings & Margins:** Earnings rebounded strongly after a volatile period. Q2 2025 net income was 5.12B (EPS 1.18), a +8.9% YoY increase. TTM net income is 18.22B. The Q4 2023 net loss of -2.57B (EPS -0.65) and weak Q3 2024 (net income 3.41B, EPS 0.78) distorted the TTM picture. Q2 2025 operating margin (operating income 8.17B / revenue 34.50B) was ~23.7%. The supplied valuation net margin is 7.6% and operating margin is 16.3%.
- **Cash-Flow:** No quarterly cash flow data is supplied for the lookback period. This is a critical evidence gap.
- **Balance Sheet:** Leverage is improving. From Q4 2023 to Q2 2025, total liabilities decreased from 286.46B to 278.92B, while total equity increased from 93.80B to 104.36B. The debt-to-equity ratio improved from 3.05x to 2.67x.

**2. Earnings Quality and Balance-Sheet Resilience**
- **Earnings Quality:** The primary concern is volatility. Quarterly EPS has swung from -0.65 (Q4 2023) to 1.18 (Q2 2025). The TTM net margin of 7.6% (supplied valuation ratio) is modest, and the Q3 2024 dip (EPS 0.78) raises durability questions. The lack of cash flow data prevents assessment of cash conversion.
- **Balance-Sheet Resilience:** The trajectory is positive, but absolute leverage remains high (liabilities 278.92B vs. equity 104.36B). Total assets have been stable (~380B).

**3. Valuation Versus Company History**
- **Current Multiples (2025-09-30):** PE Diluted = 18.095, P/B = 1.901, P/S = 1.383, Earnings Yield = 5.5%.
- **Historical Range (Oct 2024 - Sep 2025):** PE Diluted ranged from 14.9 to 19.1; P/B ranged from 1.6 to 2.0.
- **Interpretation:** The current PE of 18.095 is in the upper quartile of its 1-year range. The P/B of 1.901 is also near the top. The supplied ROE is 10.5%. Valuation is not cheap relative to its recent history.

**4. Price Level and Return Context**
- **Price Level (2025-09-30):** Close = $43.95. The 365-day range is $37.59 to $47.35.
- **Returns:** The stock has underperformed recent fundamentals. The quarterly return (to 2025-09-30) was +0.6%, while the annual return was -1.5%. The price path was volatile: a +13.5% quarterly gain into Dec 2024, followed by a -3.7% loss into Mar 2025.

**5. Strongest Quantitative Upside and Downside**
- **Upside:** The clearest positive trajectory is in top- and bottom-line growth (revenue +5.2% YoY, EPS +8.3% YoY in Q2 2025) coupled with a strengthening balance sheet (equity growth, deleveraging). If earnings stability improves, the 5.5% earnings yield could attract capital.
- **Downside:** Key risks are the high historical earnings volatility (EPS range of -0.65 to 1.18), persistent high leverage (debt/equity ~2.7x), and the valuation being at the high end of its range. The critical absence of cash flow data is a major unknown.

**6. Provisional Quantitative Risk/Reward Conclusion**
The quantitative evidence presents a mixed picture. Positive momentum in revenue and earnings growth, alongside balance sheet improvement, is countered by high leverage, historical earnings volatility, and a valuation that is not cheap on a relative (PE 18.1 vs. 14.9-19.1 range) or absolute basis (7.6% net margin, 10.5% ROE). The stock's price performance (-1.5% annual) has not rewarded the recent fundamental improvement, possibly reflecting these underlying risks. The lack of cash flow data is a significant gap. Provisionally, the risk/reward appears balanced to unfavorable, as the valuation does not appear to offer a sufficient margin of safety given the evident financial volatility and leverage.
VZ.qualitative_report:
**Qualitative Assessment Report: Verizon Communications Inc. (VZ)**
**Decision Date: 2025-10-01**

### 1. Business/Franchise Read
Verizon is a leading, integrated U.S. communications provider operating through two principal segments: **Consumer** (76% of 2024 revenue) and **Business** (22% of 2024 revenue). The Consumer segment provides wireless and wireline services, including Fios fiber and Fixed Wireless Access (FWA) broadband, to ~115 million wireless connections and ~10 million broadband connections. The Business segment offers connectivity, security, IoT, and managed network services to enterprises, public sector, and wholesale customers. The franchise is built on an extensive and reliable national wireless network, a significant fiber-optic footprint (Fios), and a large, high-quality postpaid customer base. The pending acquisition of Frontier is aimed at enhancing its fiber broadband footprint.

### 2. Recent Developments (Dated Evidence)
*   **Strategic Asset Acquisition & Network Capacity:** Verizon is in talks to acquire wireless spectrum from EchoStar (2025-09-30), a move that would bolster its network capacity. This follows reported strong investment in 5G, broadband, and AI-driven enterprise solutions (2025-08-30).
*   **Product & Partnership Innovation:** Verizon will be the first carrier to offer the new AI-powered Meta Ray-Ban Display glasses, positioning itself at the forefront of new consumer device experiences (2025-09-25).
*   **Network Leadership & Reputation:** The company continues to promote its network quality, citing 35 consecutive J.D. Power awards and RootMetrics recognition for 5G reliability and coverage (2025-09-18).
*   **Capital Allocation & Income Focus:** Multiple financial publications noted Verizon's dividend yield over 6% and expectations for a dividend payout increase in 2025, highlighting its appeal to income-focused investors (2025-09-16, 2025-09-15, 2025-09-28). The company emphasizes financial discipline (2025-08-30).
*   **Regulatory & Legal Outcome:** Verizon lost its challenge of a $46.9 million FCC fine for selling customer location data without consent, with the Second Circuit upholding the penalty (2025-09-11, 2025-09-12, 2025-09-17).

### 3. Material Qualitative Risks and Offsets
**Risks:**
*   **Regulatory/Legal Exposure:** The upheld $46.9 million fine confirms legal and regulatory liability related to data privacy and location tracking (2025-09-12, 2025-09-17). This creates reputational risk and ongoing compliance costs.
*   **Intense Competition:** The 10-K describes a "highly competitive" industry with threats from national carriers (AT&T, T-Mobile), cable MVNOs (Comcast, Charter), and technology companies offering alternative communication services. Promotional pricing is noted as a key competitive factor.
*   **Network Investment Burden:** Continuous investment is required to evolve 5G networks, densify coverage, and manage energy efficiency, amidst rising bandwidth demand.
*   **Customer Concentration in Wholesale:** A portion of Business segment Wholesale revenues come from a few large telecom companies that compete directly with Verizon.

**Offsets:**
*   **Path to Spectrum Acquisition:** Negotiations to buy EchoStar spectrum (2025-09-30), if concluded, address a key strategic need for network capacity.
*   **Network as a Moat:** Consistent recognition for network reliability and speed (2025-09-18) is a core differentiator that supports premium positioning and customer retention.
*   **Bundling Strategy:** Bundled mobile and home internet offerings are identified as increasing customer stickiness and reducing churn, mitigating competitive price wars (2025-09-15).
*   **Stable Cash Flow for Dividends:** The business generates strong cash flow supporting its high dividend yield and expected payout increases (2025-09-16, 2025-09-24), appealing to a dedicated investor base.

### 4. Qualitative Assessment
**Neutral-to-Cautiously-Positive.** The core wireless and broadband franchise remains robust, underscored by network leadership, scale, and a focus on strategic growth areas like FWA, fiber, and enterprise solutions. The potential spectrum deal supports long-term capacity needs. However, the confirmed material legal penalty and the perpetual, intense competitive pressures from cable and technology players represent clear headwinds to margin and growth. The narrative is balanced between strong foundational assets and formidable external challenges.

The 6%+ dividend yield provides income support, but the stock's -1.5% annual return despite fundamental improvement suggests the market has not rewarded the progress, and the lack of cash flow data limits conviction.

Seasonal Aspects of Operations.
Our business is seasonal to a certain extent and varies by country due to different national and religious holidays, festivals and customs, as well as different weather patterns. Historically, our highest sales volume for each segment has occurred in the fourth quarter of our fiscal year.
Suppliers and Supply Chain.
As a retailer and warehouse club operator, we utilize a global supply chain that includes both U.S. and international suppliers from whom we purchase the merchandise that we sell in our stores, clubs and online. In many instances, we purchase merchandise from producers located near the stores and clubs in which such merchandise will be sold, particularly products in the "fresh" category. Consistent with applicable laws, we offer our suppliers the opportunity to efficiently sell significant quantities of their products to us. These relationships enable us to obtain pricing that reflects the volume, certainty and cost-effectiveness these arrangements provide to such suppliers, which in turn enables us to provide low prices to our customers. Our suppliers are subject to standards of conduct, including requirements that they comply with local labor laws, local worker safety laws and other applicable laws. Our ability to acquire from our suppliers the assortment and volume of products we wish to offer to our customers, to receive those products within the required time through our supply chain and to distribute those products to our stores and clubs, determines, along with other supply chain logistics matters (such as containers or port access for example), in part, our in-stock levels in our stores and clubs and the attractiveness of our merchandise assortment we offer to our customers and members.
Intellectual Property.
We regard our trademarks, service marks, copyrights, patents, domain names, trade dress, trade secrets, proprietary technologies and similar intellectual property as important to our success, and with respect to our associates, customers and others, we rely on trademark, copyright, and patent laws, trade-secret protection, and confidentiality and/or license agreements to protect our proprietary rights. We have registered, or applied for the registration of, a number of U.S. and international domain names, trademarks, service marks and copyrights. Additionally, we have filed U.S. and international patent applications covering certain of our proprietary technology. We have licensed in the past, and expect that we may license in the future, certain of our proprietary rights to third parties.
Government Regulation.
As a company with global operations, we are subject to the laws of the United States and multiple foreign jurisdictions in which we operate and the rules and regulations of various governing bodies, which may differ among jurisdictions. For additional information, see the risk factors herein in "Item 1A. Risk Factors" under the sub-caption "Legal, Tax, Regulatory, Compliance, Reputational and Other Risks."
10
Our Shared Value Priorities
We seek to operate our business in a way that creates shared value: We believe we maximize long-term value and create competitive advantage for the Company by delivering for our stakeholders, including our customers, associates, shareholders, suppliers, business partners and communities. We believe that addressing their needs builds the value of our business, including by enhancing trust, creating new business opportunities, managing cost and risk, building capabilities for future advantage and strengthening the underlying systems on which we rely.
We prioritize issues that offer the greatest potential for Walmart to create long-term shared value: Issues that rank high in relevance to our business, importance to building and maintaining stakeholder trust and in which Walmart has the ability to be an effective actor. Our current priorities are categorized into four broad themes: opportunity, sustainability, community and ethics and integrity.
- Opportunity. We believe that promoting economic opportunity helps Walmart attract and retain the talent we need to run our business, respond to customer needs, and promote economic resilience in the places where we operate. Our approach includes creating opportunities for Walmart associates (as further described in the Human Capital Management section below), our suppliers, workers in supply chains and the communities we serve. We offer good entry-level jobs in addition to multiple career pathways; endeavor to cultivate a culture of belonging—rooted in respect for the individual—where associates and customers feel seen, supported and connected; and provide tens of thousands of local, national and global suppliers the opportunity to grow with us.
- Sustainability. Walmart's sustainability efforts endeavor to create and preserve long-term business value through increasing the efficiency and resilience of our operations, promoting surety of supply, sourcing responsibly and enhancing our product assortment. With respect to people, we aim to respect the dignity of people throughout our supply chain, create economic opportunity for people working in supply chains, and sell safer, healthier products. With respect to the environment, our efforts aim to enhance the sustainability of product supply chains; preserve, protect, and restore nature; reduce waste; procure renewable sources of energy; and reduce greenhouse gas emissions.
- Community. We believe that Walmart thrives in strong, resilient communities, and we endeavor to help communities thrive. We aim to strengthen communities by providing convenient access to affordable, quality goods and services through our omni-channel retail model and everyday low prices; contributing to the economic vitality of communities by providing quality jobs, training and career paths; investing in local suppliers; and supporting organizations and causes that matter to our customers and associates.
- Ethics and Integrity. We believe that people want to work for and do business with a company they can trust, and Walmart works to create a culture that inspires trust with our stakeholders. We seek to achieve this, by focusing on promoting ethics and compliance, governance and oversight, responsibly engaging in public policy activities, using data and technology responsibly and respecting human rights.
We periodically publish information on our shared value priorities, strategies, progress, and challenges on the environmental, social and governance ("ESG") reporting section of our corporate website and may update those disclosures from time to time. Nothing on our website, including our ESG reporting, documents or sections thereof, shall be deemed incorporated by reference into this Annual Report on Form 10-K or incorporated by reference into any of our other filings with the SEC.
Human Capital Management
Our associates – powered by technology – play a critical role in delivering on our purpose to help people save money and live better. Our business is focused on serving people and this is delivered by our approximately 2.1 million associates around the world with approximately 1.6 million associates in the U.S. and approximately 0.5 million associates internationally. In the U.S., approximately 92% of our associates are hourly and approximately 68% of our associates are full-time.
We believe our people make the difference, and we are focused on investing in the growth and well-being of our associates, investing in digital experiences to improve their quality of work and creating a culture of belonging. # WMT Quantitative Report — Decision Date 2025-10-01

## 1. Trajectory: Revenue, Earnings, Margins, Balance Sheet

**Revenue** grew steadily over the seven-quarter window: from 160.80B (2023-10-31 Q3) to 177.40B (2025-07-31 Q2). TTM revenue is 693.15B. Latest Q2 shows QoQ revenue growth of +7.1%.

**Earnings** are volatile. Quarterly EPS diluted ranges from −1.80 (2024-01-31 Q4) to 0.88 (2025-07-31 Q2). The latest Q2 EPS of 0.88 is the highest in the series; QoQ EPS growth was +57.1%. TTM net income is 21.93B. (Note: 2024-01-31 reports net_income=5.68B yet eps_diluted=−1.80 — a data inconsistency that undermines earnings-per-share trend reliability.)

**Margins** (latest Q2 2025-07-31):
- Gross margin: 44.63B / 177.40B = 25.1%
- Operating margin: 7.29B / 177.40B = 4.1%
- Net margin: 7.15B / 177.40B = 4.0% (quarterly)

The trailing net margin reported in the valuation panel is 2.8%, down from 3.6% in early 2025, indicating recent margin compression despite the latest strong quarter.

**Cash Flow:** Entirely unavailable across all reported periods. Free cash flow, operating cash flow, and cash-flow yield cannot be assessed.

**Balance Sheet** (2025-07-31): total_assets=270.84B, total_equity=96.55B. Equity has grown from 85.58B (2023-10-31) — a +12.8% increase over ~21 months. Total debt fell from 39.58B (2024-01-31) to 36.00B (2025-01-31). Debt-to-equity (latest debt data): 36.00B / 97.42B = 0.37x. Equity-to-assets: 96.55B / 270.84B = 35.6%.

## 2. Earnings Quality & Balance-Sheet Resilience

**Earnings quality** is undermined by three factors:
1. High volatility — quarterly EPS swings from −1.80 to 0.88, and from 0.17 to 0.63, within the window.
2. A stated net income/eps_diluted mismatch at 2024-01-31 (net_income=5.68B, eps=−1.80) raises data-integrity questions.
3. No cash-flow data exists to verify that reported earnings convert to cash. Free cash flow conversion is **unverifiable**.

Net margins are thin (2.8% trailing, 4.0% latest quarter) and have compressed over the valuation window from 3.6% to 2.8% per the valuation history. ROE is solid at 21.6%.

**Balance-sheet resilience** is strong: low leverage (debt/equity 0.37x), growing equity base, and declining absolute debt. No leverage red flags.

## 3. Valuation vs. Own History

| Metric | Latest (2025-09-30) | 1-Year Range | Current Position |
|---|---|---|---|
| P/E diluted | **42.575** | 28.8 – 43.1 | At ceiling |
| P/B | **9.184** | 6.8 – 9.3 | At ceiling |
| P/S | **1.211** | — | — |
| Earnings yield | **2.3%** | — | — |
| Net margin | **0.028** | 0.0 – 0.04 | Low end of range |
| ROE | **0.216** | 0.2 – 0.3 | Mid-range |

P/E has expanded ~28% (from 33.4 at 2024-10-01 to 42.6 at 2025-09-30) while net margins *declined* from 3.6% to 2.8%. This is valuation expansion on deteriorating profitability — the stock is priced at a historically expensive multiple relative to its own trailing fundamentals. The 2.3% earnings yield is thin.

## 4. Price Level & Returns vs. Fundamentals

**Latest close (2025-09-30):** 103.06
**52-week range:** 78.98 – 106.11 (price is 97.1% of the high)
**Annual return:** +29.0%
**Quarterly returns:** +16.1%, −5.4%, +11.9%, +4.9% (newest)

The stock has compounded +29.0% over the past year while trailing net margins fell ~80 bps (3.6% → 2.8%) and P/E expanded ~28%. The price trajectory has significantly outpaced the fundamental trajectory. The stock sits near its all-time high in the dataset.

## 5. Strongest Quantitative Upside & Downside

**Upside drivers (quantitative):**
- Latest Q2 EPS of 0.88 is the highest in the 7-quarter series; QoQ earnings growth of +57.1% signals an inflection if sustained.
- TTM revenue of 693.15B with accelerating QoQ growth (+7.1%) shows top-line momentum.
- Conservative balance sheet (debt/equity 0.37x) provides financial flexibility.
- ROE of 21.6% indicates efficient capital deployment.

**Downside drivers (quantitative):**
- P/E of 42.6 sits at the top of its 28.8–43.1 range — approximately 47% above the floor — leaving significant multiple-compression risk.
- Trailing net margin of 2.8% is at the low end of its window, yet the stock has never been more richly valued on P/E or P/B.
- Earnings yield of 2.3% provides minimal cushion against rising discount rates or earnings disappointment.
- Historical quarterly EPS volatility (−1.80 to 0.88) means the latest strong quarter may not be durable.
- Complete absence of cash-flow data prevents confirmation that earnings are cash-backed.

## 6. Provisional Quantitative Risk/Reward Conclusion

Risk/reward skews negatively. The stock is priced at the top of its historical valuation range (P/E 42.6 vs. 28.8–43.1; P/B 9.2 vs. 6.8–9.3) while trailing net margins have compressed (3.6% → 2.8%). The +29% annual price return has far exceeded fundamental progress. The balance sheet is sound, and the latest quarter showed a genuine earnings surge (+57.1% QoQ), but the valuation already embeds optimistic expectations — the earnings yield of 2.3% prices in significant growth. A reversion to even the midpoint of the P/E range (~35) with unchanged earnings implies ~18% downside. Upside requires sustained margin recovery above recent levels and continued revenue acceleration — conditions that must be proven over multiple quarters given the earnings volatility in the record. The quantitative posture is **unfavorable at current valuation levels**; the stock demands near-perfect execution to justify its multiple.
WMT.qualitative_report:
### Qualitative Report: Walmart Inc. (WMT)
**Decision Date:** 2025-10-01

#### 1. Business/Franchise Read
Based on the 2025-03-14 10-K filing, Walmart is a global omni-channel retailer operating under a strategy of "Everyday Low Price" (EDLP) and "Everyday Low Cost" (EDLC). The company serves ~270 million customers weekly through over 10,750 stores and eCommerce platforms in 19 countries. Its operations are divided into three segments: Walmart U.S. (69% of FY2025 net sales), Walmart International (18%), and Sam's Club U.S. (13%). The franchise is built on a foundation of price leadership, an integrated physical and digital ecosystem (including Walmart+), and expanding service offerings such as advertising, marketplace, fulfillment, and financial services. The company emphasizes continuous investment in supply chain automation, AI, and technology to enhance its omni-channel capabilities.

#### 2. Recent Developments (Dated)
*   **2025-09-16:** An article identifies Walmart as a strong candidate for reaching a trillion-dollar market cap, attributing this potential to its value-focused strategy and technological adaptations.
*   **2025-09-01:** For fiscal year 2026, Walmart expects total net sales growth of 3.75% to 4.75% and adjusted EPS growth of 0.4% to 4.4%. Growth drivers cited include rising e-commerce sales and streamlined pricing.
*   **2025-08-22:** A report details Walmart's 25% e-commerce growth and heavy investment in AI to attract top talent and improve operations.
*   **2025-08-22:** The company's CFO noted the challenge of competing for tech talent and acknowledged that profit slightly missed expectations partly due to the company's choice to absorb some tariff costs.
*   **2025-08-21:** Walmart raised its full-year earnings and sales outlook, citing double-digit online business growth, even as it reported that tariff costs are rising.

#### 3. Material Qualitative Risks and Offsets
**Risks:**
*   **Legal & Regulatory (2025-08-13, 2025-08-27):** The company faces lawsuits alleging deceptive pricing and failure to disclose material risks related to supply chain disruptions and international practices. A settlement was reached for violating California's False Advertising laws.
*   **Economic & Consumer Pressure (2025-09-16, 2025-09-22):** Household spending growth is sharply decelerating, with consumers becoming more cautious and prioritizing essentials. Tariffs are cited as a specific cost headwind for the business.
*   **Competition (from 10-K):** Walmart operates in a highly competitive and rapidly evolving landscape against brick-and-mortar, e-commerce, and omni-channel players, some of which may have advantages in brand recognition, supplier terms, or innovation.
*   **Operational Complexity (2025-09-22):** Supply chain disruptions and international business practices are highlighted as material risk factors in litigation.

**Offsets:**
*   **Scale & Logistics (from 10-K):** The company's vast physical network of over 10,750 stores and 164+ U.S. distribution facilities provides a significant structural advantage for fulfilling omni-channel demand.
*   **Strategic Investment (2025-08-22, 2025-09-01):** Walmart is actively investing in growth catalysts, including AI, automation, "Store of the Future" upgrades, and its advertising and marketplace platforms, to drive long-term sales and efficiency.
*   **Value Positioning (from 10-K, 2025-09-16):** The EDLP strategy and focus on value position Walmart favorably during periods of economic pressure and consumer caution.

#### 4. Qualitative Assessment
**Positive.** Walmart presents a robust omni-channel franchise with a clear strategic focus on leveraging technology and scale to enhance its value proposition. Recent developments show proactive investment in growth areas like e-commerce and AI, alongside a raised near-term sales outlook. The company's value orientation is a potential hedge in a slowing consumer environment. However, this positive view is tempered by tangible legal risks and the macroeconomic pressures cited in the evidence. The business appears to be actively managing its strategic evolution while navigating a complex operational and competitive landscape.

Item 1. Business
Our Company
NVIDIA pioneered accelerated computing to help solve the most challenging computational problems. NVIDIA is now a full-stack computing infrastructure company with data-center-scale offerings that are reshaping industry.
Our full-stack includes the foundational CUDA programming model that runs on all NVIDIA GPUs, as well as hundr
…[truncated 21658 chars]…
 user experience for consumer entertainment and professional visualization applications and create new virtual world and simulation capabilities. Our technologies are instrumental in driving the gaming, design, and creative industries forward, as developers leverage our libraries and algorithms to deliver an optimized experience on our GeForce and NVIDIA RTX platforms. Our computer graphics platforms leverage AI end-to-end, from the developer tools and cloud services to the Tensor Cores included in all RTX-class GPUs. For example, NVIDIA Avatar Cloud Engine, or ACE, is
7
Table of Contents
a suite of technologies that help developers bring digital avatars to life with generative AI, running in the cloud or locally on the PC. GeForce Experience enhances each gamer's experience by optimizing their PC's settings, as well as enabling the recording and sharing of gameplay. Our Studio drivers enhance and accelerate a number of popular creative applications. Omniverse is real-time 3D design collaboration and virtual world simulation software that empowers artists, designers, and creators to connect and collaborate in leading design applications. We also enable interactive graphics applications - such as games, movie and photo editing and design software - to be accessed by almost any device, almost anywhere, through our cloud platforms such as vGPU for enterprise and GeForce NOW for gaming.
Advancing the leading autonomous vehicle platform.
We believe the advent of autonomous vehicles, or AV, and electric vehicles, or EV, is revolutionizing the transportation industry. The algorithms required for autonomous driving - such as perception, localization, and planning - are too complex for legacy hand-coded approaches and will use multiple neural networks instead. Therefore, we provide an AI-based hardware and software solution, designed and implemented from the ground up based on automotive safety standards, for the AV and EV market under the DRIVE brand, which we are bringing to market through our partnerships with automotive OEMs, tier-1 suppliers, and start-ups. Our AV solution also includes the GPU-based hardware required to train the neural networks before their in-vehicle deployment, as well as to re-simulate their operation prior to any over-the-air software updates. We believe our comprehensive, top-to-bottom and end-to-end approach will enable the transportation industry to solve the complex problems arising from the shift to autonomous driving.
Leveraging our intellectual property, or IP.
We believe our IP is a valuable asset that can be accessed by our customers and partners through license and development agreements when they desire to build such capabilities directly into their own products or have us do so through a custom development. Such license and development arrangements can further enhance the reach of our technology.
Sales and Marketing
Our worldwide sales and marketing strategy is key to achieving our objective of providing markets with our high-performance and efficient computing platforms and software. Our sales and marketing teams, located across our global markets, work closely with customers and various industry ecosystems through our partner network. Our partner network incorporates global, regional and specialized CSPs, OEMs, ODMs, ISVs, global system integrators, add-in board manufacturers, or AIBs, distributors, automotive manufacturers and tier-1 automotive suppliers, and other ecosystem participants.
Members of our sales team have technical expertise and product and industry knowledge. We also employ a team of application engineers and solution architects to provide pre-sales assistance to our partner network in designing, testing, and qualifying system designs that incorporate our platforms. For example, our solution architects work with CSPs to provide pre-sales assistance to enable our customers to optimize their hardware and software infrastructure for generative AI and LLM training and deployment. They also work with foundation model and enterprise software developers to enable our customers to optimize the training and fine-tuning of their models and services, and with enterprise end-users, often in collaboration with their global system integrator of choice, to fine-tune models and build AI applications. We believe that the depth and quality of our design support are key to improving our partner network's time-to-market, maintaining a high level of customer satisfaction, and fostering relationships that encourage our customers and partner network to use the next generation of our products within each platform.
To encourage the development of applications optimized for our platforms and software, we seek to establish and maintain strong relationships in the software development community. Engineering and marketing personnel engage with key software developers to promote and discuss our platforms, as well as to ascertain individual product requirements and solve technical problems. Our developer program supports the development of AI frameworks, SDKs, and APIs for software applications and game titles that are optimized for our platforms. Our Deep Learning Institute provides in-person and online training for developers in industries and organizations around the world to build AI and accelerated computing applications that leverage our platforms.
Seasonality
Our computing platforms serve a diverse set of markets such as data centers, gaming, professional visualization, and automotive. Our desktop gaming products typically see stronger revenue in the second half of our fiscal year. Historical seasonality trends may not repeat.
Manufacturing
We utilize a fabless and contracting manufacturing strategy, whereby we employ and partner with key suppliers for all phases of the manufacturing process, including wafer fabrication, assembly, testing, and packaging. We use the expertise of industry-leading suppliers that are certified by the International Organization for Standardization in such areas as fabrication, assembly, quality control and assurance, reliability, and testing. Additionally, we can avoid many of the significant costs and risks associated with owning and operating manufacturing operations. While we may directly procure certain raw materials used in the production of our products, such as memory, substrates, and a variety of components, our suppliers are responsible for procurement of most raw materials used in the production of our products. As a result, we can focus our resources on product design, quality assurance, marketing, and customer support. In periods
8
Table of Contents
of growth, we may place non-cancellable inventory orders for certain product components in advance of our historical lead times, pay premiums, or provide deposits to secure future supply and capacity and may need to continue to do so.
We have expanded our supplier relationships to build redundancy and resilience in our operations to provide long-term manufacturing capacity aligned with growing customer demand. Our supply chain is mainly concentrated in the Asia-Pacific region. We utilize foundries, such as Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited, or TSMC, and Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd., or Samsung, to produce our semiconductor wafers. We purchase memory from SK Hynix Inc., Micron Technology, Inc., and Samsung. We utilize CoWoS technology for semiconductor packaging. We engage with independent subcontractors and contract manufacturers such as Hon Hai Precision Industry Co., Ltd., Wistron Corporation, and Fabrinet to perform assembly, testing and packaging of our final products.

## NVDA Quantitative Assessment — 2025-10-01

### 1. Revenue, Earnings, Margin, Cash-Flow & Balance-Sheet Trajectory

**Revenue:** Q2 FY26 revenue was $46.74B, up +6.1% QoQ from $44.06B and +55.6% YoY from $30.04B. Quarterly revenue has compounded aggressively from $18.12B (Q3 FY24, Oct-23) to $46.74B, a 2.6× increase over seven quarters. The QoQ growth rate of +6.1% signals deceleration from the hyper-growth phase.

**Earnings:** Q2 FY26 EPS (diluted) was $1.08, up +42.1% QoQ from $0.76 and +61.2% YoY from $0.67. *(Note: the 10-for-1 split in June 2024 renders pre-split EPS figures—$5.98, $4.92, $3.71—non-comparable in absolute terms without adjustment; YoY/QoQ growth rates are presented on a consistent basis by the source.)* Net income was $26.42B in Q2 versus $18.77B in Q1.

**Margins:** Q2 FY26 margins expanded sharply versus Q1:
- Gross margin: 33.85/46.74 = **72.4%** (vs. Q1 26.67/44.06 = **60.5%**)
- Operating margin: 28.44/46.74 = **60.8%** (vs. Q1 21.64/44.06 = **49.1%**)
- Net margin: 26.42/46.74 = **56.5%** (vs. Q1 18.77/44.06 = **42.6%**)

Cost of revenue *fell* from $17.39B (Q1) to $12.89B (Q2) despite revenue rising, which drove the margin swing. The trailing valuation-ratio net margin of 52.4% (69.8% gross) reflects this two-quarter average.

**Cash flow:** Unavailable for all reported periods. This is a material evidence gap.

**Balance sheet:** Total equity expanded from $33.27B (Oct-23) to $100.13B (Jul-25), a 3.0× increase. Total debt was essentially flat at $8.47B (vs. $9.71B in late-2023). Total assets reached $140.74B against $40.61B in liabilities, yielding a liability-to-asset ratio of 28.9%.

---

### 2. Earnings Quality & Balance-Sheet Resilience

**Earnings quality concerns:**
- The Q1→Q2 margin swing is extreme: gross margin jumped 11.9 percentage points (60.5%→72.4%) while cost of revenue dropped $4.5B on only $2.7B more revenue. This level of quarter-to-quarter cost volatility warrants scrutiny; the Q1 cost-of-revenue figure ($17.39B) stands out versus the Q2 FY25 figure ($7.47B) and Q2 FY26 ($12.89B). Without cash-flow data, the cash conversion of reported earnings cannot be verified.
- ROE declined from 0.919 (Mar-25) to **0.865** (latest), driven by a $16.3B equity increase in one quarter (Q1 equity $83.84B → Q2 equity $100.13B), suggesting retained earnings accretion rather than marginal return improvement.

**Balance-sheet resilience:**
- Debt-to-equity = **0.085** ($8.47B debt / $100.13B equity). The balance sheet is fortress-grade with minimal leverage.
- Equity exceeds total liabilities by a factor of 2.5×.
- No cash-flow or liquid-asset breakdown is available to assess short-term liquidity.

---

### 3. Valuation Versus Supplied History

| Metric | Latest (2025-09-30) | 12-Month Range | Observation |
|---|---|---|---|
| P/E (diluted) | 52.9× | 32.1× – 70.0× | Middle of range |
| P/B | 45.7× | 29.5× – 63.8× | Middle of range |
| P/S | 27.7× | — | Single point |
| Earnings yield | 1.9% | — | Low absolute yield |
| Net margin | 52.4% | 0.50 – 0.60 | Lower end of range |
| ROE | 86.5% | 0.90 – 0.90 | Below stated range floor |

The stock's P/E of 52.9× sits at the **42nd percentile** of its 12-month window (range 32.1–70.0). The trough P/E of 32.1× occurred around April 2025 when the price was near $108.38; the peak of 70.0× occurred around December 2024 near $139.93. The current multiple is neither at a discount to history nor at an extreme premium, but it prices in continued high growth at a 1.9% earnings yield.

---

### 4. Price Level & Return Context Versus Fundamentals

- **Latest close:** $186.58 on 2025-09-30, at the **52-week high** ($187.35).
- **52-week low:** $86.62, implying a **+115%** range.
- **Quarterly return:** +21.7% (Q3 2025), following +41.4% (Q2 2025), following −22.5% (Q1 2025), following +12.8% (Q4 2024). Path has been volatile.
- **Annual return:** +50.4%.

**Fundamental-price reconciliation:** Revenue grew +55.6% YoY while the stock returned +50.4% annually—price appreciation has roughly tracked revenue growth, with the P/E compressing slightly from higher levels earlier in the year. The recent quarterly price surge of +21.7% coincided with Q2 earnings where EPS grew +42.1% QoQ, so the move is supported by an earnings inflection. However, the stock is now at its 52-week high with a P/E in the middle of its range, leaving no valuation cushion.

---

### 5. Strongest Quantitative Upside & Downside

**Upside:**
- Revenue has compounded at extraordinary rates (18.12B→46.74B in seven quarters). If Q2 margins (72.4% gross, 56.5% net) are sustainable rather than a one-quarter anomaly, trailing earnings power accelerates significantly. A sustained ~$26B quarterly net-income run-rate would compress the current P/E toward the **32×** trough within 2–3 quarters at the current price, implying substantial re-rating potential.
- The balance sheet's 0.085 debt-to-equity and $100B equity base provide an unquestioned foundation; there is zero financial distress risk.

**Downside:**
- Q1 FY26 demonstrated margin fragility: gross margin was only **60.5%** (vs. 72.4% in Q2), with cost of revenue at $17.39B. If Q2 margins revert toward Q1 levels, trailing net income contracts and the P/E rises above 60×—near the top of the 12-month range (70×).
- QoQ revenue growth decelerated to **+6.1%**, well below prior quarters. If deceleration continues, the high-growth premium embedded in a 52.9× P/E comes under pressure.
- At $186.58, the stock is within 0.4% of its all-time high; with no cash-flow data to validate earnings quality, the margin volatility and unverified cash conversion represent unquantified downside risk.

---

### 6. Provisional Quantitative Risk/Reward Conclusion

NVDA presents a **high-quality but fully-valued** profile at this point-in-time snapshot. The balance sheet is exceptionally strong (debt/equity 0.085, equity $100.13B), revenue growth remains robust at +55.6% YoY, and the Q2 margin expansion to 72.4% gross / 56.5% net demonstrates powerful operating leverage. However, three factors temper conviction:

1. **Margin volatility:** The 11.9pp gross-margin swing from Q1 to Q2 (60.5%→72.4%) makes it unclear which level is the sustainable run-rate. If Q2 is the norm, the stock is reasonably priced; if Q1 is the norm, it is expensive.
2. **Valuation at midrange with price at highs:** P/E of 52.9× (midpoint of 32–70 range) while price sits at the 52-week peak means the market is pricing in continued execution without offering any multiple cushion. The 1.9% earnings yield provides negligible downside protection.
3. **Cash-flow absence:** Without any cash-flow data across seven quarters, cash conversion of $26.42B reported net income cannot be assessed—an essential missing input for valuation confidence.

**Net assessment:** The quantitative case is constructive on trajectory (revenue +55.6% YoY, earnings +61.2% YoY, minimal leverage) but neutral-to-cautious on risk/reward at the current price level and P/E. The key swing factor is whether Q2 margins or Q1 margins represent the normalized rate—an answer this dataset cannot resolve.
NVDA.qualitative_report:
### Qualitative Analysis Report: NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA)
**Decision Date:** 2025-10-01  
**Evidence Window:** From 2025-02-26 (10-K filing) through 2025-09-30 (latest news)

#### 1. Business/Franchise Description
NVIDIA operates as a full-stack computing infrastructure company with a platform strategy centered on accelerated computing. Its core architecture is built upon GPUs, CPUs (Grace), DPUs (from Mellanox acquisition), and the proprietary CUDA parallel programming model, creating a broad and deep software ecosystem that establishes a technical moat. The business is reported in two segments:
- **Compute & Networking:** Encompasses Data Center platforms (GPUs, networking, systems), automotive (DRIVE Hyperion), robotics (Jetson), and cloud services (DGX Cloud). This segment is driven by AI training and inference for hyperscalers, enterprises, and research.
- **Graphics:** Includes gaming (GeForce), professional visualization (RTX/Omniverse), and automotive infotainment.

The franchise is defined by its leadership in AI and high-performance computing, supported by a software-rich ecosystem (over 5.9 million CUDA developers) that creates high switching costs. Its platform serves diverse, multi-billion-dollar end markets (Data Center, Gaming, Professional Visualization, Automotive) using a unified underlying architecture.

#### 2. Recent Dated Developments
*   **Product & Technology (2025):**
    *   **2025-09-10:** Unveiled the "Rubin CPX" AI chip, continuing its architectural innovation pace (from Yao context).
    *   **Fiscal Year 2025 (ended Jan 2025):** Launched the Blackwell architecture for data centers, which included GPUs, CPUs, DPUs, and networking. Also launched GeForce RTX 50 Series (Blackwell) for gaming, featuring neural graphics and new DLSS technology (from 10-K).
    *   **2025-09-30:** Advanced robotics tech with a new open-source physics engine and AI reasoning model, highlighting expansion into embodied AI (from news).

*   **Demand & Investment Cycle:**
    *   **2025-09-30:** News projects tech giants will invest over $490 billion in AI infrastructure by 2026, underpinning sustained demand for NVIDIA's data center platforms (from news).
    *   **2025-09-26:** Web context cites a projected $3-4 trillion long-term AI infrastructure market and a strategic $100 billion investment partnership with OpenAI as growth drivers (from web context).
    *   **2025-09-30:** CoreWeave secured a $14.2 billion deal with Meta and a $6.5 billion expansion with OpenAI, indicating intense capital deployment into NVIDIA-powered AI infrastructure by key customers (from news).

*   **Competition & Geopolitical Risk:**
    *   **2025-09-30:** Huawei is planning to double production of its Ascend 910C AI chips to 600,000 units in 2026, backed by state support, directly challenging NVIDIA's market share in China (from news).
    *   **2025-09-15:** Chinese regulators publicly accused NVIDIA of violating anti-monopoly laws, causing a stock decline and posing a regulatory risk to its operations in a key market (from news).

*   **Regulatory & Legal:**
    *   **2025-09-02:** NVIDIA warned of legal risks related to missing regulations for a proposed U.S. 15% tariff on AI chip exports, stating it cannot make payments under current frameworks (from news).
    *   **2025-08-27:** A shareholder derivative lawsuit was disclosed in the Q2 10-Q, alleging breaches of fiduciary duty and unjust enrichment related to a prior $4.5 billion charge for H20 chips (from news).
    *   **2025-09-30:** A narrative warns that China's mineral export restrictions could threaten U.S. chip manufacturing, presenting a long-term supply chain risk (from news).

*   **Capital Expenditure & Supply:**
    *   The 10-K notes a concentrated supply chain in the Asia-Pacific region, heavy reliance on TSMC and Samsung for foundry, and a strategy of placing non-cancelable inventory orders during growth periods to secure capacity.

#### 3. Material Qualitative Risks and Offsets
**Risks:**
1.  **Geopolitical & Supply Chain Concentration:** Manufacturing is concentrated in Taiwan (TSMC) and the broader APAC region, creating vulnerability to trade tensions and political disruption. China's mineral export restrictions could exacerbate input cost and availability issues.
2.  **Competitive Threats in Key Market:** The rapid, state-backed rise of Huawei's Ascend AI chips in China directly threatens NVIDIA's leadership position in a large market, potentially eroding market share and pricing power.
3.  **Regulatory Entanglement in China:** The anti-monopoly accusations from Chinese regulators create a material risk of fines, operational restrictions, or forced technology transfers.
4.  **U.S. Trade Policy Uncertainty:** Proposed and uncertain export tariffs (e.g., the 15% AI chip fee) could disrupt supply chains and increase costs; NVIDIA's warning suggests it cannot yet operate compliantly.
5.  **Customer Concentration:** Heavy dependence on a few hyperscale cloud providers (evident from deals like CoreWeave's $14.2B Meta contract) creates demand volatility if a single large customer alters purchasing plans.
6.  **Legal Exposure:** Pending shareholder litigation creates a risk of financial liability and management distraction.

**Offsets:**
1.  **Dominant Software Ecosystem (CUDA):** The extensive developer base (5.9M+) and deep software stack create a significant moat, making it difficult for competitors (like Huawei or custom ASICs) to displace NVIDIA even with comparable hardware.
2.  **Full-Stack Platform Advantage:** NVIDIA's integrated offering (hardware, networking, systems, software) provides superior performance and ease of deployment, which is critical for complex AI workloads. This is evidenced by its products being run on "every major cloud provider and server maker."
3.  **Expanded Product Breadth:** The move into CPUs (Grace), DPUs, and networking (Mellanox) diversifies its revenue base within the data center and makes its platform more indispensable.
4.  **Structural AI Demand Growth:** The multi-trillion-dollar projected investment in AI infrastructure by major tech firms provides a strong secular tailwind for the entire sector, which NVIDIA is best positioned to capture.
5.  **Diversified End-Market Exposure:** Leadership in Gaming, Professional Visualization, and Automotive markets provides horizontal diversification beyond the cyclical Data Center boom.

#### 4. Qualitative Assessment
**Assessment: Positive, with elevated risks.**

The qualitative evidence strongly supports a positive view of NVIDIA's franchise. Its technical moat, built on CUDA and a full-stack platform, is deep and evidenced by an unmatched ecosystem and continuous innovation (Blackwell, Rubin). The demand environment is robust, with massive capital flows into AI infrastructure directly benefiting its core business.

However, the near-term and medium-term risks are non-trivial and have increased in prominence. The intensifying competitive threat from Huawei in China, coupled with active regulatory hurdles there and legal uncertainties in the U.S., represent material headwinds. The stock's advance to 52-week highs (as of 2025-09-30) likely prices in the significant growth opportunity, leaving it sensitive to setbacks on any of these risk fronts.

**Conclusion:** NVIDIA's dominant technological position and the structural demand for AI infrastructure form a compelling positive narrative. The recent developments confirm continued product momentum and massive customer investment. The primary qualitative offsets to this positive view are geopolitical (supply chain, China market access) rather than fundamental competitive threats in the open market, though Huawei's progress warrants close monitoring. The net qualitative assessment is positive.
